extra.reasoning:
The property (monotonicity in pallor) is VIOLATED. pallor appears in exactly one rule: interaction_drop (priority 100, the highest), whose antecedent is (confusion AND rash AND dizziness AND pallor) and whose consequent sets acuity to self_care, the LOWEST acuity. Because pallor only gates this dominant downgrade rule, setting pallor true can newly enable it and override higher-acuity outcomes.

Counterexample pair (toggle pallor with everything else fixed at confusion=true, rash=true, dizziness=true, syncope=false):

With pallor=false: rules that fire are raise_rash (prio 3 -> emergent), raise_dizziness (prio 2 -> urgent), raise_confusion (prio 2 -> urgent). interaction_drop does NOT fire (pallor false). Highest priority among fired rules is raise_rash (prio 3), so acuity = emergent.

With pallor=true (the reported witness): raise_rash (prio 3 -> emergent), raise_dizziness (prio 2 -> urgent), raise_confusion (prio 2 -> urgent), and now interaction_drop (prio 100 -> self_care) fires. The highest-priority matching rule is interaction_drop (prio 100), so acuity = self_care.

Setting pallor true lowers acuity from emergent to self_care, the maximum possible drop, directly violating the requirement that pallor never lowers acuity. The witness_assignment records the pallor=true input producing self_care; its pallor=false counterpart produces emergent.
